name: Garden tomato (Solanum lycopersicum), red ripe raw (food medium)

components.name: water
components.evidence: Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average; ~94.5 g water/100 g.

components.name: D-glucose
components.evidence: Glucose (dextrose) 1.25 g/100g; a co-dominant fermentable carbon source.

components.name: D-fructose
components.evidence: Fructose 1.37 g/100g; slightly exceeds glucose in ripe fruit — the two reducing sugars dominate soluble solids.

components.name: sucrose
components.source: USDA FDC 170457 / Aoun et al. 2013 doi:10.5897/AJFS2013.1067
components.evidence: Present only in trace amounts in cultivated tomato; not detected/quantified by USDA. Glucose+fructose are the fermentable sugars, not sucrose.

components.name: citric acid
components.source: Aoun et al. 2013 doi:10.5897/AJFS2013.1067 / Davies & Hobson 1981
components.evidence: Dominant organic acid of red ripe tomato; typical red-ripe content ~0.35 g/100g (literature range ~0.25-0.73 g/100g across cultivars). Titratable acidity in Aoun averaged ~4 g/L citric-acid equivalent. A major carbon/energy source for microbes — the key metabolite USDA omits.

components.name: malic acid
components.source: Davies & Maw 1972 J Sci Food Agric 23:969 / Davies & Hobson 1981
components.evidence: Second organic acid; declines during ripening so red fruit is fairly low (~0.05-0.15 g/100g), citric > malic in red ripe fruit.

components.name: L-ascorbic acid (vitamin C)
components.evidence: Third acidic constituent; also vitamin C. 13.7 mg/100g.

components.name: gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA)
components.evidence: Present at ~20-80 mg/100g FW in red ripe fruit; accumulates at green stage then declines during ripening. Approximate typical red-ripe value ~0.05 g/100g.

components.name: oxalic acid
components.source: Davies & Hobson 1981 Crit Rev Food Sci 15:205
components.evidence: Minor organic acid, ~5 mg/100g; included for completeness.

components.name: L-glutamate
components.evidence: Glutamic acid 0.431 g/100g (total); the most abundant amino acid, largely free glutamate (umami), a preferred N/C source.

components.name: L-aspartate
components.evidence: Aspartic acid 0.135 g/100g; second most abundant, largely free.

components.name: L-asparagine
components.evidence: A major free amino acid in tomato fruit alongside glutamate; present but not quantified by USDA (folded into total N).

components.name: sulfate (sulfur)
components.evidence: Sulfur present (sulfate + S-amino acids); needed for growth, not separately quantified by USDA.

components.name: chloride
components.evidence: Chloride present as a counter-ion; not quantified by USDA.

components.name: vitamin C (ascorbate)
components.evidence: Total ascorbic acid 13.7 mg/100g (also listed above as organic acid).

components.name: glycine betaine
components.evidence: Betaine present in low amounts (~0.1 mg/100g); genuine osmolyte.

components.name: linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: 0.080 g/100g; dominant fatty acid of the small lipid fraction.

components.name: dietary fiber (pectin / cellulose)
components.evidence: Total dietary fiber 1.2 g/100g; includes pectin (galacturonic-acid polymer, 'pectic acid') and cellulose/hemicellulose from cell walls.

remove.name: Silver
remove.reason: non-nutritive trace contaminant, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Arsenate
remove.reason: toxic metalloid contaminant, not a nutrient; FooDB trace

remove.name: Cadmium
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Lead
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Mercury
remove.reason: toxic heavy-metal contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: non-nutritive trace element; FooDB noise, not a defining tomato nutrient

remove.name: Co2+ / cobalt
remove.reason: trace element tied to B12 which tomato lacks; FooDB predicted noise

remove.name: Molybdate
remove.reason: ultra-trace; not a growth-relevant tomato nutrient at quantified levels; FooDB noise

remove.name: Boron
remove.reason: plant micronutrient but not a microbial growth nutrient at trace levels; FooDB noise

remove.name: 3-Methylbutanal
remove.reason: volatile aroma compound at negligible mass; not a growth-relevant nutrient

missing_added: citric acid (0.35 g/100g) — dominant organic acid, the key USDA omission
individual sugars glucose (1.25) and fructose (1.37) with real amounts
full vitamin panel (B1/B2/B3/B5/B6/B9, C, E, K, beta-carotene, choline)
keep_uncovered: lycopene (dominant carotenoid, real phytochemical, no BiGG met)
pectic acid (cell-wall pectin, counted under fiber)
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 170457 (Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average)
Aoun, Lechiheb, Benyahya & Ferchichi 2013, Afr. J. Food Sci. 7(10):350-354, doi:10.5897/AJFS2013.1067
Davies & Maw 1972, J. Sci. Food Agric. 23:969-979 (citric/malic metabolism during ripening)
Davies & Hobson 1981, Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Technol. 15:205-280 (constituents of tomato fruit)
Agius et al. 2018, MethodsX / Heliyon PMC6046607 (sugars & organic acids quantification)
MDPI Agronomy 2021 11(6):1204 (GABA content of tomato cultivars)
notes: Red ripe raw tomato is a dilute, watery matrix (~94.5% water). Dominant carbon/energy sources for microbial growth are the two reducing sugars (glucose ~1.25 + fructose ~1.37 g/100g) and citric acid (~0.35 g/100g, the dominant organic acid and the main gap in the FooDB/USDA-only view), with lower malic acid (~0.10 g/100g). Free glutamate and aspartate are the leading amino/N sources; potassium is the major mineral. Twenty-one FooDB entries were removed as non-nutritive trace metals/contaminants or volatiles; genuine phytochemicals (phenolics, flavonoids, lignans, lycopene, tomatine) are retained as uncovered.
